alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Model cards provide benchmarked evaluation of machine learning models across various cultural, demographic, and phenotypic groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model cards provide benchmarked evaluation of machine learning models across various cultural, demographic, and phenotypic groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result claim is carried forward verbatim, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework of model cards can be applied to document any trained machine learning model, not just those in computer vision and natural language processing. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework can be applied to document any trained machine learning model, not just those in human-centered applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication rephrases the domain exceptions (from CV/NLP to human-centered applications) but maintains the same general applicability claim, rendering it a clarification rather than a substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduction of model cards is a step towards the responsible democratization of machine learning and AI technology. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model cards aim to increase transparency regarding the performance of artificial intelligence technology. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the intent more modestly (transparency) versus the preprint's broader normative claim about democratization, indicating a narrowed and tempered claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The lack of established mechanisms and standards leads to the underutilization of data generated by smart cities. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The lack of established mechanisms and standards leads to the underutilization of data generated by smart cities. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same high-level conclusion without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Semi-supervision is essential for smart cities to effectively utilize the unlabeled data generated. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Semi-supervision is essential for smart cities to effectively utilize the unlabeled data generated. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This central conclusion is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A three-level learning framework is proposed to match the hierarchical nature of big data in smart cities. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed three-level learning framework is scalable and aligns with the hierarchical nature of big data in smart cities. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds that the framework is scalable while retaining the original contribution claim, so it clarifies and slightly strengthens the description.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework utilizes semi-supervised deep reinforcement learning to improve the performance of smart city services. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework utilizes a mix of labeled and unlabeled data to improve control policies and reduce waste of unlabeled data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the mechanism (mix of labeled/unlabeled) and specific effects (improving control policies, reducing waste) with higher certainty, clarifying and strengthening the preprint result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The paper identifies challenges and future research directions for integrating machine learning into smart city services. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include the explicit identification of challenges/future directions, so this preprint claim is not carried forward.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep reinforcement learning can enhance the cognitive capabilities of smart city services by shifting towards semi-supervision. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This specific concluding statement about deep RL enhancing cognitive capabilities via semi-supervision appears in the publication but was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The routing operation determines the certainty with which a layer of capsules passes information to the layer above, and the appropriate level of certainty is related to the model fitness. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The routing operation determines the certainty with which a layer of capsules pass information to the layer above and the appropriate level of certainty is related to the model fitness. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed Sparse-to-Continuous technique demonstrates a significant improvement in depth map quality for outdoor scenes. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel densification method for depth maps utilizes the Hilbert Maps framework to produce a continuous occupancy map from LiDAR scans. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep Confidence can be applied to any deep learning-based application at no extra computational cost. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The learned matching criterion is integrated within a metric-free optimization framework based on graphical models. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Learned multi-metric registration outperforms single-metric approaches based on conventional similarity measures. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Over 95% of Quizlet users report improved grades as a result of using the platform. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimal data releasing mechanism is characterized through convex optimization under the assumption of linear transformations by both data holders and attackers. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Effective numerical algorithms can approximate unknown governing differential equations from measurement data.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using a large number of short bursts of trajectory data is more effective for accurate approximation than using data from a single long trajectory.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The properties and effectiveness of the equation approximation algorithms are demonstrated through extensive numerical examples.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed methodology for nonlinear inverse problems effectively uses a variational Bayesian approach to handle spatial fields. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed methodology for nonlinear inverse problems using a variational Bayesian approach effectively captures epistemic uncertainty from limited input and output examples. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The structured Bayesian Gaussian process latent variable model captures epistemic uncertainty and automatically selects an appropriate dimensionality for the learned latent representation. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methodology demonstrates improved sample efficiency and computational tractability through the use of Kronecker product decompositions of relevant kernel matrices. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The structured Bayesian Gaussian process latent variable model improves sample efficiency by leveraging spatial information for an informative generative prior. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication emphasizes the sample-efficiency aspect and frames it in terms of leveraging spatial information; the core claim about improved sample efficiency is preserved though details (Kronecker decompositions) are downplayed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methodology is shown to return well-calibrated posteriors and is tractable with latent spaces exceeding 100 dimensions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The methodology is demonstrated on an elliptic PDE and is shown to return well-calibrated posteriors. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The methodology is shown to return well-calibrated posteriors and is tractable with latent spaces exceeding 100 dimensions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach is tractable with latent spaces containing over 100 dimensions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The tractability-with-large-latent-spaces claim in the preprint directly matches this publication claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Our algorithm outputs an ℓ2-norm sample from a rank-k approximation of a matrix in time O(poly(k)log(mn)), only polynomially slower than the quantum algorithm. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Kerenidis and Prakash’s algorithm does not in fact give an exponential speedup over classical algorithms. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The classical recommendation system resulting from our algorithm produces recommendations exponentially faster than previous classical systems, which run in time linear in m and n. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same quantitative performance claim retained in publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of simple routines to manipulate $\text{l}^2$-norm sampling distributions indicates a potentially fruitful framework for formally comparing quantum machine learning algorithms to classical machine learning algorithms. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This correspondence indicates a potentially fruitful framework for formally comparing quantum machine learning algorithms to classical machine learning algorithms. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: We reveal the existence of two regimes characterized by the presence or absence of a critical point for the gradient in cellular decision-making models. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: On the Kinetics dataset, TSD+I3D requires only 50% of the number of frames compared to I3D to achieve almost the same accuracies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: On the Kinetics dataset, TSD+I3D typically requires only 50% of the number of frames compared to I3D to achieve almost the same accuracies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds the qualifier "typically," softening the absolutist phrasing; meaning preserved but slightly tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TSD has a lightweight architecture that can be deployed on mobile devices to produce compressed representative frames, saving transmission bandwidth. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific claim about mobile deployment and lightweight architecture does not appear in the published claim list and thus appears removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TSD significantly reduces the computations required to run video action recognition with compressed frames on the cloud while maintaining high recognition accuracies. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TSD significantly reduces the computations required to run video action recognition with compressed frames on the cloud while maintaining high recognition accuracies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same but the publication presents it with stronger certainty/result framing (medium -> high), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.pub_claim_text: TSD can be plugged in as a preprocessing module of any existing 3D CNNs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This claim about plug-and-play usage is new in the publication and has no counterpart in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed deep learning architectures effectively combine text information with time-series data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep learning architectures effectively combine text information with time-series data for forecasting. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the contribution with consistent meaning and certainty (minor wording adds forecasting context).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Fusing text information with time-series data significantly reduces the error in taxi demand forecasts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Fusing text information with temporal data significantly reduces forecasting error in taxi demand predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same empirical result expressed with synonymous wording and identical certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Modern forecasting techniques typically neglect valuable information available in unstructured text. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Modern forecasting techniques typically neglect valuable information available in unstructured text. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Limitation claim is preserved verbatim between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed methodology combines Tabu search and Gradient descent with momentum backpropagation to optimize the architecture of Feedforward neural networks. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The new algorithm combines the advantages of Tabu search and Gradient descent with a momentum backpropagation training algorithm. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Core methodological combination is the same in both versions; phrasing differs but content and certainty are unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new methodology can automatically select the best architecture for Feedforward neural networks based on minimum testing error criteria. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed methodology can simultaneously optimize the number of hidden layers and their respective neurons for deep feedforward neural networks. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies what aspects of architecture are optimized (layers and neurons), narrowing the general "select best architecture" claim while retaining high certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach has been tested on four classification benchmark datasets of different sizes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimized networks demonstrate better generalization ability when tested on four different classification benchmark datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication augments the testing statement with an explicit positive performance claim (better generalization) and increases certainty, so the original testing-only claim is revised and strengthened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Under the affine independence assumption, ASSC is guaranteed to produce subspace-preserving affinity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Under the affine independence assumption, ASSC is guaranteed to produce subspace-preserving affinity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical technical claim and certainty in publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subspace-preserving recovery can be achieved under much weaker conditions for all data points other than the extreme points of samples from each subspace. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Subspace-preserving recovery can be achieved under much weaker conditions for all data points other than the extreme points of samples from each subspace. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim preserved verbatim with same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The affinity produced by ASSC may be subspace-dense, which could guarantee the subspace-preserving affinity of ASSC to produce correct clustering under rather weak conditions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The affinity produced by ASSC may be subspace-dense, which could guarantee the subspace-preserving affinity of ASSC to produce correct clustering under rather weak conditions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The nuanced theoretical claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed convolutional neural network-based approach effectively identifies the source smartphone for printed text documents captured by smartphone cameras.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method outperforms baseline methods for the classification of WhatsApp-processed document images.
alignments.rationale: Both claims assert successful source-smartphone identification for document images, but the publication frames results specifically for WhatsApp-processed images (narrower) and emphasizes outperforming baselines, which clarifies the original claim's context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experiments demonstrate that the proposed system performs as well as or better than the state-of-the-art system based on handcrafted features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method outperforms baseline methods for the classification of WhatsApp-processed document images.
alignments.rationale: The publication casts the comparison more strongly (outperforms) and limits evaluation to WhatsApp-processed images, changing the original 'as well as or better' phrasing into a firmer superiority claim in a narrower setting.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A new image dataset consisting of 315 images of documents printed in three different fonts has been introduced for the source smartphone identification problem.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A new image dataset consisting of 770 images of documents printed in three different fonts has been introduced.
alignments.rationale: Both claims describe the same new dataset (three fonts) but the publication reports a different (larger) image count, so the contribution is preserved but the dataset size is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The new dataset and code of the proposed system will be made publicly available upon publication of this letter.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not state that the dataset and code will be made publicly available, so this commitment from the preprint is absent in the published version.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The method utilizes a fusion technique that allows a single network to learn a model from two-channel images.
alignments.rationale: This methodological detail about a two-channel fusion technique does not appear in the preprint and is new in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Experiments were conducted in the presence of an active adversary who might re-scale the native images before sharing.
alignments.rationale: The adversary-rescaling experimental condition is not mentioned in the preprint and thus represents an added detail in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed Bayesian hierarchical model identifies communities in networks without directly observing the edges.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed Bayesian hierarchical model identifies communities of time series.
alignments.rationale: The core modeling contribution is the same, but the publication explicitly frames the application to time series whereas the preprint stated a more general 'networks without observed edges', narrowing the stated domain.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model provides an end-to-end community detection algorithm that propagates uncertainties from the raw data to the community labels.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model provides an end-to-end community detection algorithm that propagates uncertainties from the raw data to the community labels.
alignments.rationale: The claim is essentially verbatim between preprint and publication, preserving meaning, scope, and expressed confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach supports multiscale community detection and the selection of an optimal scale using model comparison.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach supports multiscale community detection and the selection of an optimal scale using model comparison.
alignments.rationale: The multiscale detection and model-comparison selection claim is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The properties of the algorithm were studied using synthetic data and applied to daily returns of constituents of the S&P100 index as well as climate data from US cities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The properties of the algorithm were studied using synthetic data and applied to daily returns of constituents of the S&P100 index and climate data from U.S. cities.
alignments.rationale: The empirical evaluation described is the same in both versions, with only minor stylistic changes in phrasing (U.S. vs US).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed matching framework integrates local descriptors for multimodal registration and demonstrates superior matching performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed matching framework demonstrates superior matching performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same core result about superior matching performance of the proposed framework.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel pixel-wise feature representation, channel features of orientated gradients (CFOG), outperforms traditional pixel-wise HOG descriptors in both matching performance and computational efficiency.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The novel pixelwise feature representation, named channel features of orientated gradients (CFOG), provides superior performance in image matching.
alignments.rationale: Both assert CFOG's superior performance; the publication emphasizes superior matching performance but omits the explicit HOG comparison and efficiency claim in this specific sentence, so the claim is slightly clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework achieves high computational efficiency due to the use of the 3 Dimensional Fast Fourier Transform (3DFFT) technique.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of fast Fourier transform (FFT) in the proposed framework contributes to high computational efficiency.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the efficiency claim tied to FFT use but uses a more general 'FFT' phrasing rather than explicitly '3DFFT', clarifying/softening the specific technique named in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework has been implemented in software products of a Chinese listed company.
alignments.rationale: The published paper does not mention corporate deployment or implementation in a listed company's products, so this contribution is absent.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework integrates local descriptors for multimodal images to address the challenges of significant nonlinear intensity differences.
alignments.rationale: While related to the framework, the explicit statement about addressing significant nonlinear intensity differences in multimodal images appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed symmetric line segments (SLS) generator effectively addresses the challenges of scale and direction variations in airplane detection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed symmetric line segments (SLS) generator effectively addresses the challenges of scale and direction variations in airplane detection.
alignments.rationale: The contribution is stated identically in both preprint and publication, preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The modified loss function in the deep convolutional neural network allows for effective learning from multiple instance bags of airplane proposals.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The modified loss function in the deep convolutional neural network allows for effective learning from multiple instance bags of airplane proposals.
alignments.rationale: The technical claim about the modified loss and MIL learning is preserved verbatim between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experiments on the NWPU VHR-10 and DOTA datasets demonstrate that the proposed method is a promising approach for automatic airplane detection in very high-resolution images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experiments on the NWPU VHR-10 and DOTA datasets demonstrate that the proposed method is a promising approach for automatic airplane detection in very high-resolution images.
alignments.rationale: The empirical result and datasets reported are the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method also enables the estimation of airplane direction using box-level annotations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method also enables the estimation of airplane direction using box-level annotations.
alignments.rationale: The directional-estimation capability using box-level annotations appears unchanged between preprint and published paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The paper presents a novel workflow for classifying spaceborne TomoSAR point clouds into buildings and non-buildings using auxiliary information.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The paper presents a novel workflow for classifying TomoSAR point clouds into buildings and nonbuildings using auxiliary information.
alignments.rationale: The contribution is the same; the publication's wording is equivalent to the preprint's claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The workflow produces automatic large-scale benchmark labelled SAR datasets for building detection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The labeled data sets generated from the workflow have been used to train state-of-the-art deep fully convolutional neural networks for building detection in VHR SAR images.
alignments.rationale: Both concern labeled datasets from the workflow; the publication emphasizes their use to train FCNs, clarifying the datasets' downstream use relative to the preprint's focus on dataset generation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The labelled datasets have been used to train state-of-the-art deep Fully Convolution Neural Networks for detecting building regions in VHR SAR images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The labeled data sets generated from the workflow have been used to train state-of-the-art deep fully convolutional neural networks for building detection in VHR SAR images.
alignments.rationale: This result about using the generated labeled datasets to train FCNs is retained verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The building detection method achieved mean pixel accuracies of around 93.84% over a TerraSAR-X VHR spotlight SAR image of Berlin.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method achieves a mean pixel accuracy of around 93.84% for building detection in a TerraSAR-X VHR spotlight SAR image.
alignments.rationale: The published result reproduces the same accuracy figure and dataset context as reported in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The order bias in user surveys can be significantly reduced by randomizing the display order of tokens. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This selection bias can be significantly reduced by randomizing the display order of tokens. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same claim that randomizing display order significantly reduces selection/order bias with equivalent emphasis and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Users respond to the randomized-order variant at levels that are comparable to the fixed-order variant. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Users respond to the randomized-order variant at levels that are comparable to the fixed-order variant. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim, so there is no substantive change in content, scope, or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The effective selection of a subset of token questions is achieved by extracting tokens that provide the highest information gain over user ratings. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The effective selection of a subset of tokens is achieved by extracting tokens that provide the highest information gain over user ratings. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same methodological principle (select by information gain); differences are terminological only (contribution vs conclusion).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The selection of token questions is known to be in the class of NP-hard problems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This selection is known to be in the class of NP-hard problems. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions assert the same computational-complexity classification without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Applying a greedy submodular maximization method allows capturing 94% of the information using just 30% of the questions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this specific quantitative result about greedy submodular maximization (94% with 30%), so the claim is absent in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed exact passive-aggressive online algorithms can be used with interval labels instead of actual labels for examples. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed exact passive-aggressive online algorithms for ordinal regression can be used with interval labels instead of actual labels. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this contribution verbatim with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed algorithms maintain the ordering of the thresholds after every trial. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed algorithms maintain the ordering of the thresholds after every trial. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim in publication; no change in meaning or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mistake bounds of the proposed algorithms are provided in both ideal and general settings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mistake bounds of the proposed algorithms are provided in both ideal and general settings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The paper keeps the same theoretical claim about mistake bounds with equal emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed algorithms successfully learn accurate classifiers using both interval labels and exact labels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed algorithms successfully learn accurate classifiers using both interval labels and exact labels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental/theoretical claim is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed algorithms perform well compared to other approaches. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed algorithms perform well compared to other approaches. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance claim remains the same; wording and confidence unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed method for automatic detection of mitotic figures in breast cancer tissue sections demonstrates a significant improvement over manual counting, which is time-consuming and error-prone. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed method using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can automatically detect mitotic figures in breast cancer tissue sections. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the automatic-detection claim but drops the explicit comparative claim of significant improvement over manual counting, making the claim weaker and narrower in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The combination of image analysis of mitotic activity in PHH3 restained slides and registration allows for the creation of a reference standard for mitosis detection in H&E WSIs with minimal manual annotation effort. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A reference standard for mitosis detection in entire H&E whole-slide images was built with minimal manual annotation effort. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both versions describe building a reference standard with minimal manual annotation; the publication preserves the contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The data augmentation strategy designed in this study creates diverse and realistic H&E stain variations, resulting in a stain invariant mitosis detector. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The data augmentation strategy created diverse and realistic H&E stain variations, resulting in a stain invariant mitosis detector. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim about augmentation and stain invariance without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Knowledge distillation was successfully applied to reduce the computational requirements of the mitosis detection ensemble with negligible loss of performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Knowledge distillation was applied to reduce the computational requirements of the mitosis detection ensemble with negligible loss of performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same claim about knowledge distillation and its negligible performance loss.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system achieved performance within the top-3 best methods for most tasks of the Tumor Proliferation Assessment Challenge (TUPAC) when evaluated on an independent multicenter cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The system achieved performance within the top three best methods for most tasks of the tumor proliferation assessment challenge. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the competitive performance on the TUPAC challenge; the meaning and confidence are preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We propose a new method using deep learning for automatically segmenting epithelial tissue in digitized prostatectomy slides. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A new deep learning method was proposed to segment epithelial tissue in digitised hematoxylin and eosin stained prostatectomy slides. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same contribution (deep learning segmentation of epithelial tissue) with equivalent scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our system makes precise cell-level segmentations and segments both intact glands as well as individual (tumor) epithelial cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The system accurately segmented both intact glands and individual tumour epithelial cells. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions claim accurate segmentation of glands and individual tumour epithelial cells; content and confidence are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We achieved an F1-score of 0.895 on a hold-out test set and 0.827 on an external reference set from a different center. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generalisation capacity of the system was demonstrated using an independent external dataset from a different centre. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes demonstration of generalisation on an external dataset but omits the specific numeric F1 values, hence the claim is clarified to focus on generalisation and slightly narrowed in detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We envision this segmentation as being the first part of a fully automated prostate cancer detection and grading pipeline. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The segmentation method is envisioned as the first part of a fully automated prostate cancer grading pipeline. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same forward-looking conclusion about the segmentation forming part of an automated grading pipeline.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed rotatable region-based residual network (R$^3$-Net) effectively detects multi-oriented vehicles in aerial images and videos. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed rotatable region-based residual network (R3-Net) effectively detects multioriented vehicles in aerial images and videos. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core result that R3-Net effectively detects multioriented vehicles with equivalent wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: R$^3$-Net utilizes a rotatable region proposal network (R-RPN) to generate rotatable regions of interest (R-RoIs) from feature maps. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: R3-Net utilizes a rotatable region proposal network (R-RPN) to generate rotatable region of interests (R-RoIs) from feature maps. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The methodological contribution (R-RPN generating R-RoIs) is restated in the publication without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The novel rotatable position sensitive pooling (R-PS pooling) in R-DN maintains position and orientation information while downsampling feature maps. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not mention this specific R-PS pooling component or its properties, so the claim about this novel pooling is absent in the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experiments demonstrate the high precision and robustness of the R$^3$-Net method on the DLR 3K Munich Dataset and VEDAI Dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrates high precision and robustness when tested on the DLR 3K Munich Data set and VEDAI Data set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental demonstration on the same datasets with the same conclusions is preserved in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method shows good generalization capability for vehicle tracking in aerial videos. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further experiments indicate that the proposed method has good generalization capability for vehicle tracking in aerial videos. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the conclusion about generalization capability for vehicle tracking with similar confidence and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CAVP can attend to complex visual compositions over time, improving image captioning performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CAVP can attend to complex visual compositions over time, improving image captioning performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim is textually identical and expresses the same result and certainty as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed Context-Aware Visual Policy network (CAVP) explicitly accounts for previous visual attentions to enhance word generation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed Context-Aware Visual Policy network (CAVP) explicitly accounts for previous visual attentions to enhance word generation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement is the same in the publication with no substantive changes in content or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CAVP achieves state-of-the-art performances on the MS-COCO offline split and online server. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CAVP achieves state-of-the-art performances on the MS-COCO offline split and online server. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance claim is identical between preprint and publication, with no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Existing RL-based image captioning methods fail to capture crucial visual context for compositional reasoning. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Existing RL-based image captioning methods fail to capture crucial visual context for compositional reasoning. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is carried over verbatim into the publication with the same emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The COCO benchmarking suite has established itself as an important platform for exchanging research ideas in continuous optimization. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The COCO benchmarking suite has established itself as an important platform for exchanging research ideas in continuous optimization. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement about COCO is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We adjust the COCO software to pseudo-Boolean optimization problems, creating a benchmarking environment for discrete black-box heuristics. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The adjustment of the COCO software to pseudo-Boolean optimization problems creates a benchmarking environment for discrete black-box heuristics. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the adjustment to COCO for pseudo-Boolean problems with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The test bed can be used to profile the performance of evolutionary algorithms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The test bed can be used to profile the performance of evolutionary algorithms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about the test bed's use for profiling EAs is present unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimization behavior of several $(1+\beta)$ EA variants is studied on the benchmark problems OneMax and LeadingOnes. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimization behavior of several (1 + ) EA variants was studied on the benchmark problems OneMax and LeadingOnes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same study results but uses firmer certainty (high vs medium) and slightly different wording; content is the same but confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This comparison motivates a refined analysis for the optimization time of the $(1+\beta)$ EA on LeadingOnes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The specific follow-up conclusion about motivating refined analysis does not appear in the publication's listed claims and is therefore removed from the claim set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed architecture achieves 91.16% classification accuracy for the MNIST dataset and 90% accuracy for the EUNF dataset, even in the presence of noise. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed architecture achieves 91.16% classification accuracy for the MNIST dataset and 90% accuracy for the EUNF dataset, even in the presence of noise. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the numeric accuracy results and noise robustness claim exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The temporal memory sequence prediction demonstrates 95% accuracy for a 5-number long sequence generated from the EUNF dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The temporal memory sequence prediction achieves 95% accuracy for a 5-number long sequence generated from the EUNF dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The sequence prediction accuracy claim is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed hardware architecture offers a 1364X speedup over the software realization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed hardware architecture offers a $1364 \times$ speedup over the software realization. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported large speedup is carried into the publication with equivalent wording (formatting differs but meaning is identical).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed architecture can serve as a digital core to build the HTM in hardware and eventually as a standalone self-learning system. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed architecture can serve as a digital core to build the HTM in hardware and eventually as a standalone self-learning system. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The prospective conclusion about serving as a digital HTM core remains present and unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed algorithm creates high resolution anatomically plausible images from clinical brain MRI scans with large inter-slice spacing. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed algorithm creates high resolution anatomically plausible images from clinical brain MRI scans with large inter-slice spacing. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The core contribution about producing high-resolution anatomically plausible images is retained verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The generative model captures fine-scale anatomical structure across subjects in clinical image collections. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The generative model captures fine-scale anatomical structure across subjects in clinical image collections. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The textual claim about capturing fine-scale anatomy is preserved; the publication labels it as a result rather than contribution but the substantive claim is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The experimental results demonstrate that the method outperforms state-of-the-art upsampling super-resolution techniques. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The experimental results demonstrate that the method outperforms state-of-the-art upsampling super-resolution techniques. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental superiority claim is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method promises to facilitate subsequent analysis not previously possible with scans of this quality. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method promises to facilitate subsequent analysis not previously possible with scans of this quality. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The prospective benefit for downstream analysis is maintained with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed framework for hand-eye calibration of an ophthalmic robot with a microscope-integrated Optical Coherence Tomography (MIOCT) operates without any markers. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed framework for hand-eye calibration of an ophthalmic robot with a microscope-integrated optical coherence tomography (OCT) operates without any markers. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The markerless hand-eye calibration framework claim is carried over intact; differences are only stylistic (acronym formatting).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean calibration error of the proposed method was 9.2 μm, which is comparable to the marker-based method with a mean error of 7.0 μm. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method demonstrated a mean calibration error of 9.2m compared to a reference marker-based method with a mean error of 7.0m. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric calibration results are the same; the publication appears to have a unit-formatting issue but the underlying comparative result is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method demonstrated robustness as indicated by the noise test results. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The noise test indicates the robustness of the proposed hand-eye calibration method. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same robustness finding from the noise tests with equivalent meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed degridding methods yield consistent improvements on the performance of dense prediction tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experimental results show that the proposed methods yield significant and consistent improvements on the performance of DCNNs with dilated convolutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same empirical improvement but frames it more specifically (DCNNs with dilated convolutions) and emphasizes significance in addition to consistency, thus clarifying and narrowing the original claim while amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SS output layer significantly improves performance and is very efficient in terms of the number of training parameters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed methods add negligible amounts of extra training parameters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both texts assert improved performance and emphasize very small/negligible extra parameters; the publication sharpens the parameter-efficiency wording but retains the same substantive claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The two degridding approaches are intrinsically related and define separable and shared (SS) operations. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The two degridding approaches are intrinsically related and define separable and shared operations that generalize the proposed methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the relation between the two approaches but adds that these operations generalize the methods, clarifying and broadening the original conceptual claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed methods address gridding artifacts by smoothing the dilated convolution itself. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed degridding methods effectively address the gridding artifacts by smoothing the dilated convolution itself. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same mechanistic claim but states it more strongly and with higher certainty, so the content is unchanged while confidence is amplified.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Dilated convolutions suffer from gridding artifacts that hamper the performance of deep convolutional neural networks. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The explicit statement of the limitation (gridding artifacts harming DCNN performance) appears in the publication but was not separately listed among the preprint claims, so it is an added explicit limitation claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: StressNet results in significantly lower prediction errors than SCSNet on three loss functions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: StressNet results in significantly lower prediction errors than SCSNet on three loss functions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with the same confidence and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean relative error for testing using StressNet is 2.04%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean relative error for testing using StressNet is 2.04%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical quantitative result presented in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models may offer a promising alternative to classical methods in structural design and topology optimization. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models may offer a promising alternative to classical methods in structural design and topology optimization. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding suggestion appears unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed hybrid approach of quantum information processing for quantum linear regression utilizes both discrete and continuous quantum variables. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed hybrid approach of quantum linear regression utilizes both discrete and continuous quantum variables. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Data information is encoded in a qubit system while information processing is tackled using auxiliary continuous qumodes via qubit-qumode interactions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Data information is encoded in a qubit system while information processing is handled using auxiliary continuous qumodes. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Finite squeezing is helpful for efficiently running quantum algorithms in realistic setups. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Finite squeezing is helpful for efficiently running quantum algorithms in a realistic setup. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hybrid approach is more efficient and feasible for implementing quantum algorithms compared to an all-qubit approach, while still retaining exponential quantum speed-up. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The hybrid approach is more efficient and feasible for implementing quantum algorithms compared to an all-qubit approach. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The hybrid approach retains exponential quantum speed-up. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the retention of exponential quantum speed-up as a separate explicit claim; while this content was present in the preprint's combined claim, it appears as a distinct added publication claim in the finalized paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This article presents a taxonomy of the field that explores and matches algorithm strategies by extracting similarities and differences in their search strategies. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This article presents a taxonomy of the field that explores and matches algorithm strategies by extracting similarities and differences in their search strategies. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The taxonomy provides a deeper understanding of the components of search strategies and indicates the close connections between different algorithm designs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
